sentences.text: Yes, the Russian-- actually not Russia, the Soviets, yes?
They cross the border, yes-- the so-called Curzon Line, you know, it was in the West Ukraine, yes.
And from [NON-ENGLISH] it was, I moved to Rowne, it was a small city.
It Rowne, and around Rowne, you know, there was a lot of Czech people, you know.
They move from Bohemia where is from the Czech, in the last century to Russia-- it belongs to Russia.
They started on the farms, there.
And one condition was they must change their religion from Catholics, there was [NON-ENGLISH], you know-- Russian orthodox, you know.
So I come there and started work in a farm, you know.
It was September, October, you know.
I tell you, the trouble started after-- I don't know how many weeks.
There [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how you say it-- [NON-ENGLISH] it was the interior police, you know-- the political police.
They took us all around, you know, we was many hundred people around this town, Rowne, where we work, you know.
And the first thing they take from us, the documents.
They said-- they told us, you are illegal in the Soviet Union.
So I said, how we are illegal?
You crossed the border, not we.
So I say, no, you are illegal, you don't have permission to stay in the Soviet Union.
Said, you know, you have seen, we have a Czechoslovak passport.
Say, yes, you have a Czechoslovak passport, but Czechoslovakia don't exist-- exist only protectorat Bohemia Moravia, and the Slovak independent state.
So you are stateless, you know.
And what I don't know before never, you know, in Soviet Union exist Jewish nationality.
So they accept I am Jewish nationality, you know, but not citizenship, yeah?
I was without citizen-- only the nationality.
So they take out from us the passport, and give us some piece of paper, with the dates.
Nationality-- [NON-ENGLISH], In Russia you say [NON-ENGLISH], yes?
It is only in Russia, yes-- here, it is, you can be Israeli-- Jewish religion.
But there is Jewish nationality, you know.
I never know with this.
So I worked there on the farm, it is Czech, so I go to eat, and everything.
Did they pay you?
I don't remember.
It was important to give us to eat, and you have-- you can sleep, normally, so I work on the farm.
No, no, no, Czech.
90% from all these many hundreds of people was Jewish, you know.
It was order for the Russians.
So I worked there on the field.
You know, I don't know how you say this-- there was the sugar roots, you know.
So I take the sugar roots to the factory.
And the factory-- from the factory, the owner, he told me where I [INAUDIBLE].
So I was a strong young man, you know-- I took on my-- this-- 50 kilos, this, and take it like this.
So you can see, I work very hard.
So there, we have been up to-- I don't remember-- Januar or Februar.
And these then, the Russian, the Soviets, they collect us all, and took us in a train, you know.
Not carriages, there was-- it call it eight horses and 40 mens, you know-- in the train and took us to Russia.
So-- What was that journey like?
How did you feel?
You can-- you can imagine.
We have been in this train, 40 people, not to sleep, no toilet, not water, you know.
Always-- when they stop somewhere, we run out to go to a toilet, or something, you know.
For the men, it wasn't so difficult.
For the girls, it was a little bit difficult.
There was mostly young people, you know, around their 30s-- so between mid-40s, so.
So, they took us in the train through the Ukraine, yes, to Rostov, a town on the Azov River.
Yes, and from there, I don't remember again.
They take us to the north, and stopped in a small town.
And they took us in many groups.
So 20, 30 these.
So this journey from Rowne, on the west- east Ukraine, there it need nearly three weeks.
You can imagine how we enjoyed three weeks in this train.
And there-- Did anybody die in the train?
I don't remember-- I think, yes.
No doctor, nothing, you know.
No.
Oh, food, yes.
They give us food-- mostly, sometime after 24 hours, they stop somewhere, and they give us-- there's a special Russian food, this borscht.
It's like a soup with the meat, and all around.
So I can't say we have been so hungry-- no, we don't eat enough, you know.
And there, they put us in the town.
It was so-- primitive caravan.
They put us in, 30 people.
And again, it was on the River Don, they took us to the River Volga.
This journey was three or four days.
You know-- it was in winter.
There was in a caravan, it was pulled by a tractor, you know-- a caterpillar tractor, you know.
Always, we stop somewhere over night-- yes-- in a small village, and put us through the night, and the early morning again.
So we come in the state farm after four years-- four days.
It was a state farm-- it was a state farm.
Before the war, you know, it belongs to the czar aristocrats farms, separated.
It was actually three or four private farms in one, you know.
So I started work.
It was the winter time and uh-- you know where they repair the tractors and cars and machines, you know?
So we start that work there, up to [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] up to March, you know.
And after March, I had a driving license from Czechoslovakia, so I started working on the tractors.
Not the Caterpillar tractors with the V's, you know, on the fields.
And they were practically-- it was a forced-- forced labor, though we was more or less free.
They give us in the room, we sleep in the room, I don't know, 15, 20 men, you know.
No toilet, no water.
In winter, it was minus 20 grade-- you know, you must get from the first floor out in the rear from the house to the toilet.
You can imagine this pleasure, yes?
So I work there.
Did they give you clothes to wear?
What?
What I have.
And after they give us-- it was a special, I don't know how you tell it in-- it was like an anorak, you know?
A fitted anorak they give us, you know.
But I was lucky, you know.
I had a watch, you know.
Everybody no money, so a watch, in Russia, it was a big deal.
So I sold the watch.
So I had always a little bit money, you know, to buy something-- more food or this.
So, how I say, in this state farm it was forced labor, you know.
No more or less we was free in this square-- you can go nothing.
You have no documents, not these.
So we was working there.
It was better than somewhere in Siberia, you know?
Did you ever think of escaping?
Trying to escape?
Where you can escape?
There are 100 kilometers to the nearest-- nearest town, you know?
So it's difficult.
They don't worry about that we escape or something.
You know-- we can go for a walk, and first of all, you don't have a document.
And secondly, no transport.
So there was no doctor-- it was a so-called [NON-ENGLISH], it is a you know here, how you call the people what go with the ambulance and give the first help.
It's not a doctor.
You know, and he comes once in a week, one in a fortnight, you know, around, around.
All the help was when you have something wrong with the teeth-- they pull out the teeth.
Nobody comments, nothing.
So there I was working, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was '40, '41, yes?
And they give us free-- when we would go away.
So I try go to Lugansk, it was [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
Don't ask the trip-- it was difficult, we walk, with a lorry, and so.
I don't understand-- they let you go?
They let me go.
Well, they have nothing to do, you know.
No it was beginning-- the summer.
Not so many.
Not always, they don't like-- why, I'll tell you.
When we come to [NON-ENGLISH] I have the document, you know-- without citizenship, Jewish nationality.
It was difficult to find a job.
So mostly from us stayed there, and come back to the farm.
It was for us better to stay on the farm than go in the town.
We had work.
So there we start-- was up to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1941, and started the war between the Soviet Union and Germany, you know.
So what happens?
All the German go very quickly through the Ukraine, and started occupy it from Leningrad, Moscow, and the Russian part.
So, they required us to the Volga River-- in this time it call it Stalingrad.
Before the war, it was Petrovgrad, you know, and [NON-ENGLISH], I don't remember.
So they evacuate us.
Partly we walk, partly, partly they take us on horses, and [INAUDIBLE] cars, you know.
How many people were evacuated?
How many people, roughly?
I don't-- a lot of people, including immigrants, you know, the Jewish people.
So they stay in Stalingrad for a few days, sleep outside-- and under control from the police, you know.
And wait for a ship to take us with the Volga River.
So I think it was three, four days comes the ship-- with other prisoners, you know, from the north, and so.
Nothing.
You sit in the corner and wait, wait, wait.
Hundreds.
It wasn't a place to-- you can sit, only sit on the floor, you know.
No, there was Soviet criminals, a lot, you know.
So you must everything hold [LAUGHS] very, this.
So we stopped in Azov in Astrakhan, and this for wait for the other ship to go to Krasnovodsk, you know, to Asia.
So in this Astrakhan we eat, three times in a day, fish soup.
Fish in the morning, fish soup in the lunch, fish soup in the evening.
You know, the fish goes here out.
Not a piece of bread, not a potato.
You can imagine what it was.
You can buy nothing.
So it is what-- now I don't like fish.
My wife, she like fish, she buy fish.
I don't, from this time, I don't like fish.
After three days, comes the other ship.
They took us to Krasnovodsk, to the central Asia, you know.
And from there, they took us with the train need maybe four or six weeks.
You know-- with the train to central Asia.
From Azerbaijan, Tashkent, to Tajikistan.
There's nothing to tell.
They take us, we were run two, three, days, you know.
After the stop, where the other train, it was going, we stayed in some station two, three days, again.
They take us a few hundred kilometers, and so on.
You know?
In the carriage, so, always 30, 40 people, you know, in the carriage.
No, it was a normal-- not a person carriage.
It was for goats, you know?
It wasn't bad.
It was [NON-ENGLISH], no winter, you know.
It was in summer-- summer time.
Huh?
Yes, they gave us food.
No, not so much.
What?
I don't remember, exactly.
Oh it was friends what I know from-- what we lived together, you know?
So we finished in Tajikistan-- you know, in the Soviet Republic, Tajikistan.
And there was, again, troubles with the work.
So they give me-- I started work in a brick and tiles factory.
You know?
So I work manual-- I always, this immigration, I work in manual.
It was interesting when I was there.
They don't have people in the office, you know?
So I work partly manual, and partly they take me-- I spoke Russian-- to make-- help in the office.
You know, I don't know you know this-- now we have calculators, yes?
In this time, when I started work, in 1941 there, there wasn't calculators.
It was this to count, you know?
You know what I mean?
It is so [? shares, ?] yes?
One, tens, hundreds, thousands, so always you put.
So I help in the office.
A very interesting thing was there.
When I work, there were forced labor people, I work with a Tajik-- you know, I was thinking, he's a Tajik.
What happens?
We started to talking, I come from Europe, I am a Jewish, yes?
And he tell me, you know, I am a Jewish, too.
And this-- what happens to you, and all of Europe people, it was a punishment from God, why you run away from the religion.
I say, how you can talk like that?
Yes, I am a Jewish.
I am from one from the 12 Jewish this-- very religious, no different religious.
Not like the-- like the Jewish from Europe.
So he said it was a punishment from God-- that Hitler was a punishment to the Jewish from God.
So I worked there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 19-- end of-- beginning 1942, or so.
How old were you at this point, Albert?
Oh, around 28, so you know.
So started, you know, the government, the [INAUDIBLE] government in London, you know, started making agreement with the Soviets.
So the Soviets accept the existence of Czechoslovakia, occupied by Germany-- but the government was in London.
You know?
And this time, I heard, they start Czechoslovak army, you know, in Russia-- they give permission to-- before us was the Polish army, you know?
The Sikorsky [INAUDIBLE].
And they started this.
So I go to the police, you know, the interior.
I will go to the army.
You are not a Czechoslovak, you are a stateless Jewish-- this.
You can't go.
I said, yes, I heard it in the radio, or somewhere somebody told us from this, they exist, and I am entitled to go.
He said, why you don't go to the Czechoslovak army, you can go in the Russian army, I said, I am not a Russian.
You told me I am without citizenship, when you took me in the army.
So this to allowed me to go in the army, I'd need 10 months to give me permission to go to the army.
It was from Dushanbe, yes, Stalinabad, to Buzuluk, under the Uran.
To come to the army, it needs nearly three weeks in train.
It was more or less a normal train, normal carriages, you know.
No, it wasn't so difficult.
First of all, they give us some eat, you know, to take with us.
And I had money-- you know, what I sell my shirt, shoes and watch.
So I wasn't short in money, luckily.
So I come to Buzuluk.
Yes, some Czechoslovak boys, yes.
I don't remember their names.
Around five-- I don't remember, exactly.
You know?
So I come to Buzuluk, you know.
The first thing, when I was the army, they cut the hair.
Why?
We are full of-- how you call?
No, these small these louses, so.
Yes, they cut the hair.
Huh?
Yes, it was wintertime, spring-- it was too cold.
So I take my this on the head, you know.
So I was in Buzuluk, I must make all their [INAUDIBLE] these-- so they give me back my [INAUDIBLE] this In 19-- They knew you were a sergeant?
What?
Yes, I must make exams, and witnesses, and this.
In Buzuluk, in the army.
They take us for-- not only me-- for six weeks exercise, and like that.
So after six weeks, they give me back my-- [TAPS SHOULDER] this.
Oh, in Buzuluk, it was more or less [INAUDIBLE].
First of all, you can normally [INAUDIBLE] eat, you know?
What wasn't normal was sleep or 50, 60 peoples in a room, you know?
Without on the floor-- not on the floor, on desk, and so.
So, it was, again, you know, the toilet, you must go 50 or 100 yards out.
You know, so, troubles.
Very cold is difficult to say.
It's no different if you have 10- minus 10 centigrade, or 25.
So far it's no windy, and you have something on the ear, it is no different.
Here, is different-- it is wet.
No, there was very dry.
You know, it freezes, snow, dry, winter.
